title: The Beatitudes, Part 2
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
outline: Focus: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven (v.3)
What does it mean to be poor in spirit?
The kingdom of heaven as the reward
summary: Second lesson in the Beatitudes Sunday School series. Elder Nick Anderson continues from Part 1 and focuses on the first Beatitude: ‘Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.’ Examines what spiritual poverty means in the biblical context and how it opens the door to the kingdom of God.
transcript:
## Review: The Background of the Sermon on the Mount

The Sermon on the Mount is the longest and most comprehensive sermon we have recorded in the Bible, covering blessings, money, worry, ethics, marriage, relationships, prayer, tithing, fasting, the way of salvation, the fruit of salvation, and wisdom. Christ was the greatest preacher who ever lived — intelligent, powerful, successful in all He preached, and a lawful preacher. His pulpit was a mountain.

We have been considering what it means to be *blessed*. Calvin in his commentary translates the first word of every Beatitude as *happy*: *“Happy are the poor in spirit.”* This is not a worldly happiness based on circumstances — a good day, a raise, something pleasant happening to you. It is a deeper happiness, an abiding bliss that fills the heart with joy even in the most dire of circumstances. It is worth pursuing. Jesus was the happiest person who ever lived, and He is the portrait of the Beatitudes.

We have also seen that *blessed* is not the same as materially prosperous. God blessed covenant Israel in the Old Testament with land, grain, oil, military success, and peace — but those were corporate blessings for covenant Israel, not promises to the church. If you are living in the United States, you are rich. And being materially rich carries a great responsibility. Solomon wrote in Ecclesiastes:

> *“He who loves silver will not be satisfied with silver; nor he who loves abundance, with increase. This also is vanity.”* (Ecclesiastes 5:10)

A shorter definition of blessing: **that which makes a person closer to Christ is a blessing**.

## What It Means to Be Poor in Spirit

> *“Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.”* (Matthew 5:3)

Being poor in spirit does not mean being physically poor, being spiritually unsaved, being emotionally downcast, or making yourself poor by taking a vow of poverty. Thomas Watson defines it: *“Those who are brought to the sense of their sins, and seeing no goodness in themselves, despair of themselves and sue wholly to the mercy of God in Christ.”* That is the person who is poor in spirit.

Calvin says: *“The poor in spirit are those who see nothing in themselves but fly to mercy for sanctuary.”*

This is the foundation of everything that follows in the Beatitudes. Christ begins here because everything else is built on this. **No one can be saved without becoming poor in spirit**, and this is a work that God does in the hearts of His people.

## Why Poverty of Spirit Is Essential

**Without it we cannot receive grace.** If we are swollen with pride and think we have no need of Christ, we will trust in ourselves.

**Without it we cannot see how precious Christ is.** His death will be meaningless to us. This is the tragedy behind those who have deconstructed from the faith — they have no sense of what Christ actually did to save His people.

**Without it we cannot go to heaven.** No one trusting in himself will enter the kingdom of heaven. The tax collector said: *“God, be merciful to me a sinner”* — and he was saved (Luke 18:13). Paul said he desired to *“be found in Him, not having my own righteousness which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ”* (Philippians 3:9). These are examples of those who knew they could not trust in themselves and trusted Christ instead.

## Marks of Those Who Are Poor in Spirit

The poor in spirit are **weaned from themselves** — like John the Baptist who said, *“He must increase, but I must decrease.”*

The poor in spirit are **admirers of Christ**. They see all that Christ is, all that He has, all that He can do — and they see themselves as nothing by comparison.

The poor in spirit **long for a better spiritual life**. They want to know Christ. They want to know their faults and strive against them. And strangely, the more a person grows in Christ, the more he sees his own sinfulness and the more he wants to repent.

The poor in spirit are **lowly in heart** — not proud, not arrogant, not haughty. They say with Job: *“I abhor myself.”*

The poor in spirit **pray**. The person who does not pray is not poor in spirit, because the poor in spirit feels his need so deeply that it drives him to pray.

The poor in spirit are **content** — not with the world, but with Christ. Paul says: *“Having food and clothing, with these we shall be content”* (1 Timothy 6:8).

These marks are always imperfect this side of glory. But we should experience them at least some of the time, and when we do not, we repent and fight for them again.

## The Promise: Theirs Is the Kingdom of Heaven

The promise to the poor in spirit is *the kingdom of heaven* — primarily the glory the saints will experience for all eternity.

Consider perspective. Someone might live a hundred years. How long is that compared to eternity? We can suffer a little in this life knowing we will be with Christ forever and that it will never end. Christ endured the cross because of the joy set before Him — He was thinking about eternity and about us. We need the same perspective.

In that kingdom the poor in spirit will **reign with Christ forever**, without sin, without Satan, without death. As Revelation 2:10 says: *“Be faithful until death, and I will give you the crown of life.”* As 1 Peter 5:4 says: *“When the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that does not fade away.”*

This kingdom is a **perfect kingdom** — its builder and maker is God (Hebrews 11:10). It is a **secure kingdom** — every enemy will be utterly subdued. It is an **everlasting kingdom** — it will never pass away.

Thomas Watson wrote: *“God does not make us work and give no reward. Godliness enthrones us in a kingdom. Whatever we go through in this life, whatever we have to do as God’s people, it will all be worth it.”*

## Conclusion

Joel Osteen says your best life is now. He is wrong. Your worst life is now — and even that is a glorious thing, because what awaits us in Christ is beyond comparison.

Ask yourself: What do you think about most? What do you spend your time and money on? What are you striving to become? Walk worthy of the kingdom of God. You stand to inherit a crown, a kingdom, and a robe. See this world as a world that is not worthy of your deepest affections — because it is not. To be poor in spirit is to be spiritually rich. You will inherit the kingdom of heaven.
